insight: In base set with Thunderhead available, threatening or buying Thunderhead early is a strong opening strategy; double blue into double red as soon as you have Thunderhead threat is competitive.
author: consensus (mqp, punf, String)
context: Discussion of base set opening strategies and Thunderhead's strength.

insight: In base set, P1 going third Engineer with no other attacking units available loses to P2's natural Animus build; introducing any green unit or smooth unit like Doomed Drone or Vendium Scrap changes the meta so third Engineer becomes correct.
author: 307th (supported by mtanzer, punf)
context: Debate on base set optimal openings and the role of third Engineer.

insight: Rhino spam (early Rhino purchases) prevents the opponent from freely droning and mirroring Tarsiers; it is an effective counter to early Tarsier builds in base set.
author: steel0229e (tested vs 307th)
context: Testing of P1 offensive strategies in base set against P2's Tarsier strategy.

insight: Fast Animus followed by 2 Tarsier every turn is a reasonable strategy in many sets, but if the opponent mirrors it, you should eventually cut Tarsiers and pivot to defense.
author: mqp
context: Player asking how to beat Tarsier spam strategy.

insight: In most sets, going for a random attacker should beat fast Tarsier spam; if you're new, memorizing Tarsier lines may be easier but the strategy is not inherently overpowered.
author: Deleted User
context: Discussion of why Tarsier spam appears unbeatable to new players.

insight: Tarsiers are stronger attackers in base set than Inmolite, especially early game; Inmolite is only more efficient if it forces immediate defense or when offensive finesse matters significantly.
author: mrguy888
context: Comparison of attack unit efficiency in early turns.

insight: The first Xenonbus is overpowered as an absorber, but all subsequent Xenonbus and absorbers in general are weak; Xenonbus is inefficient as an attacker or blocker.
author: mrguy888
context: Balance evaluation of Xenonbus unit.

insight: Building lines with inefficient investments in attack units (like Steelsplitter and Gauss Cannon as primary attackers) fail to make efficient use of resources; focus on efficient attackers or clear economic advantage.
author: mrguy888
context: Critique of a proposed P1 opening line in base set.

insight: The standard P1 opening in base set is DD|DD|DDA|TTD; earlier Animus leaves you on very low economy and makes it hard to get Blastforge and Walls without wasting Red.
author: mtanzer
context: Discussion of optimal base set P1 opening.

insight: Rhino has a significant absorb advantage over Tarsier when it absorbs even 1 damage (e.g., turn 3); in simple scenarios, Rhino should outperform Tarsier.
author: mqp
context: Analysis of Rhino vs Tarsier in a theoretical base set scenario.

insight: Base set only is the simplest unit pool; adding even a single unit to the pool drastically changes timings and interactions. Random set complexity increases exponentially with more units, making base set theory only theoretically relevant.
author: steel0229e
context: Context on why obsessing over base set balance is not practical for real games.

insight: With base+Zemora+Vivid, P1 starting with 5 Drones and 1 Gold (vs P2 with 6 Drones) can win by playing T1: 1 Conduit instead of DD, leveraging Zemora's fast tech advantage.
author: crash_overlord (tested)
context: Testing of alternative starting positions to explore edge cases in Prismata opening theory.

insight: Zemora Voidbringer with Wall-only defense beats Tarsier-based attacks even without Synthesizer; Doomed Wall provides stronger early defense than regular Wall in Zemora sets.
author: crash_overlord (tested)
context: Testing Zemora as a defensive unit against early rushes.

insight: Redeemer is effective when: (1) there is good cheap soak available for your defense, and (2) your opponent cannot easily buy counter damage to make the charges hurt significantly. Avoid Redeemer against fast Red units.
author: wilson0862 (supported by Deleted User, mundungu)
context: Guide for identifying when Redeemer is the correct unit to build.

insight: Redeemer is very effective against Wild Drone due to fast damage forcing the opponent to build defense, causing Wild Drones to become ineffective or die to Redeemer's charges.
author: mrguy888, Deleted User
context: Discussion of how Redeemer counters Wild Drone strategies.

insight: Redeemer is strong against high-absorb defensive units because the defender cannot easily block all the charges; Redeemer pairs well with absorbent units like Plexo Cell that reduce charge damage.
author: Deleted User, wilson0862
context: Analysis of Redeemer's role in high-absorb environments.

insight: Redeemer is generally not optimal in sets with attack-cost units like Lancetooth because those units make it hard to fully deny absorb, which is what makes Redeemer efficient.
author: wilson0862, Deleted User
context: Discussion of why Redeemer underperforms in specific unit pool contexts.

insight: When planning to buy Redeemer early, Conduit is a natural second-turn purchase to enable the Redeemer line as early as possible.
author: punf
context: Brief comment on Redeemer opening sequencing.

insight: According to high-level player Weiseguy, a good heuristic for buying Redeemer is to ask: do I have 8GGB and want more attack? If yes, Redeemer is likely correct.
author: mqp (quoting Weiseguy)
context: General heuristic for Redeemer timing.

insight: P1 and P2 starting drone counts are equal (27 drones each) as of May 6, 2017 patch; before that P1 had 26 and P2 had 27, creating a 1-drone deficit for P1.
author: jamesa7171 (citing patch notes)
context: Correction of assumption about P1/P2 starting resource imbalance.
